𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirty — Part Two

The Damascus Document

SEREKH DAMMESEK

The Laws — The Exact Interpretation of the Torah

Columns 9 through 16

No Man Shall Defile His Holy Spirit — The Well Is the Torah — The Inspector Shall Love Them as a Father Loves His Children — The Covenant of Creator Stands Fast Forever — Life Forever

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — Cairo Geniza MS A and B — 4QD — 5QD — 6QD

DSS Primary Authority — No Christian or Rabbinic Framework

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

THE DAMASCUS DOCUMENT — THE LAWS — COLUMNS 9 THROUGH 16

DAMASCUS DOCUMENT — COLUMN 9 The Laws Begin — And This Is The Rule — Concerning Oaths — Two Witnesses Are Required — Concerning the Inspector — Whoever Is Found to Have Lied Shall Be Excluded — No Man Shall Bring a Charge Before the Assembly Without Witnesses

1 And this is the rule of oaths. If a man swears to do what is according to the Torah or to abstain from anything, let him not redeem it even at the cost of death. Neither shall he approach to swear by Aleph and Lamed or by Aleph and Daleth but only by the oath of the covenant.

2 And if he swears by the covenant and then departs from it, he has profaned the name and shall be cut off from the congregation.

3 And everything that a man may devote to Creator from the property of his household must be done according to the custom of the priests, and cannot be revised or altered.

4 Whatever he devotes from his field, the priest shall take it and eat it. And if he devotes the field permanently, it shall belong to the priests. And if the owner of the field wants to redeem it, the priest shall accept whatever price he offers.

5 And all the firstfruits of the honey, the firstfruits of the wheat and of the wine and of the oil, the first of all things which they offer to Creator, belong to the priests. And the tithe of cattle and sheep belongs to the priests.

6 But the tithe of the grain and the wine and the oil, these the priests must distribute according to the exact interpretation of the Torah. And none of these shall be taken from the hands of the owners by force.

7 No man shall bring a charge against his neighbor before the assembly unless he has first rebuked him in the presence of witnesses. And if anyone is found to have lied, he shall be excluded from the pure food of the congregation for six months.

8 This is the rule concerning the charge of a capital offence. If a charge is established by two witnesses or by three witnesses, the matter is confirmed. But if there is only one witness, he shall not accuse.

9 No one shall be believed as a witness against his neighbor for a capital offence on the basis of his own statement, for it is written: You shall not be put to death on the testimony of one witness.

10 Nor shall a man be believed for any sin or crime on his own word alone. And the witness who witnesses against his neighbor shall first rebuke him in private and then bring the matter before the Inspector with the witnesses.

11 The Inspector shall receive the report from them. And if a man commits a sin through ignorance, the witnesses shall report it to the Inspector immediately. And the Inspector shall record it.

12 And if he does it again before the Inspector there shall be a second witness whose report shall be recorded. And when the count of his offences is complete, the case shall be judged according to the ordinance.

13 If two witnesses testify about the same matter but not about the same time — if the man is faithful in the essentials of the covenant, the first witness shall be accepted even if the time differs — for the essential testimony is the same.

14 But no man shall accuse anyone of a matter involving the death penalty unless there are two witnesses. And in all matters of the Torah, no accusation shall be brought except by two or three witnesses.

15 And the Inspector who is in the camps shall be between thirty and fifty years of age, master of every secret of men and of every tongue of their various nations. According to his instruction the members of the congregation shall come in and in his order they shall go out.

16 He shall investigate every grievance of a man against his neighbor. And he shall instruct the congregation in the works of Creator and make them understand his mighty deeds and recount to them the everlasting events with their interpretations.

17 And he shall love them as a father loves his children and shall carry them in all their distress like a shepherd carries his flock. And he shall loosen all the bonds which restrict them so that no one who is oppressed or afflicted shall be in the congregation.

18 Everyone who enters the congregation, the Inspector shall examine him concerning his works, his understanding, his strength, his might, and his wealth. And he shall inscribe him in his place according to his share in the lot of truth.

19 And no man of the members of the covenant of the community shall trade with the sons of the Pit except hand to hand. And no man shall form a partnership with them unless it is for ready money.

20 And no man shall take anything from them except for a price. And if one takes anything from them it shall be as the buying of a slave from a stranger.

21 And everyone who enters the covenant of the community shall be examined each year by the Inspector, whether his works have been faithful according to the covenant or whether he has gone astray. And they shall be ranked each man above his neighbor according to his understanding and the perfection of his walk.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran 4QD fragments confirm Column 9, which opens the Laws section of the Damascus Document. The transition from Admonition to Laws marks a shift in the document's character: from historical and theological exposition to concrete covenant community legislation. The covenant two-witness rule of verses 7-14 reflects the covenant Torah's covenant requirement of Devarim 17:6 and 19:15, applied with the covenant community's covenant characteristic covenant precision to the specific covenant disciplinary structures of the covenant camp and congregation.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez covenant legal tradition preserves the covenant two-witness requirement as a covenant foundational covenant judicial principle. The Damascus Document's covenant elaboration of this covenant principle — requiring prior private rebuking before public accusation, requiring the Inspector to record all covenant charges, and specifying the covenant time relationship between covenant testimonies — reflects the covenant careful covenant legal covenant development characteristic of a covenant community that takes covenant justice as seriously as covenant ritual covenant purity.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 17:6 and 19:15 provide the covenant foundational covenant legal covenant texts for the covenant two-witness rule of Column 9. The Damascus Document's covenant specific covenant application — that no one shall be believed on his own word alone for a capital offence — extends the covenant Mosaic covenant principle into the specific covenant institutional covenant structures of the covenant Inspector's covenant office and the covenant congregation's covenant deliberative process.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15-17 — the Inspector shall be between thirty and fifty years of age, master of every secret of men and of every tongue of their various nations. He shall love them as a father loves his children and shall carry them in all their distress like a shepherd carries his flock. The covenant pastoral covenant description of the Inspector — mevakker — is one of the most beautiful covenant passages in the Damascus Document's Laws section: the covenant administrative covenant role is inseparable from the covenant pastoral covenant care that must accompany it.

DAMASCUS DOCUMENT — COLUMN 10 The Shabbat Laws Begin — Let No Man Do Work on the Sixth Day — No Man Shall Walk More Than a Thousand Cubits — No Man Shall Carry Anything on the Shabbat — No Man Shall Travel on the Shabbat — No Man Shall Fast Voluntarily on the Shabbat

1 And this is the rule for the session of the many: each man shall be seated in his place. And the priests shall be seated first and the elders second and the rest of all the people shall be seated each man according to his rank.

2 And thus shall they be consulted about any matter. And the priest who is appointed at the head of the many shall be examined as to his understanding, and he shall speak to the many. And afterward the elders shall speak.

3 And no man shall interrupt the words of his neighbor before his brother finishes speaking, and none shall speak before his rank. And the man who is asked shall speak in his turn.

4 And in the council of the many, none shall speak a word except the one whose place it is to speak. And if a man has something to say to the council of the many, let him stand up and say: I have something to say to the many.

5 And these are the exact interpretations of the Torah for the Shabbat day, to observe it as Creator commanded: Keep the Shabbat day to sanctify it.

6 No man shall do work on the sixth day from the time that the sphere of the sun is distant from the gate by its own fullness. For this is what he said: Observe the Shabbat day to sanctify it.

7 And on the Shabbat day no man shall speak a vain or foolish word. No man shall lend anything to his neighbor. No man shall bring a judgment concerning property or gain.

8 No man shall speak about matters of labor and work to be done the next day. No man shall go out into the field to do the work of his work on the Shabbat day.

9 No man shall walk more than one thousand cubits beyond his city. No man shall eat anything that had not been prepared before the Shabbat. And no man shall eat or drink except what was prepared in the camp.

10 But if he is on a journey and goes down to wash, let him drink where he stands. But he shall not draw water into a vessel. No man shall send the son of a stranger to do his business on the Shabbat day.

11 No man shall put on dirty garments or garments put in storage unless they were washed with water or rubbed with incense. No man shall observe a voluntary fast on the Shabbat day.

12 No man shall go after an animal to pasture it outside his city more than two thousand cubits. No man shall raise his hand to strike it with a fist. If it is stubborn, let him not take it out.

13 No man shall carry anything from the house to the outside or from the outside to the house. Even if he is in a booth, let him not carry out anything or bring anything in from there.

14 No man shall open a sealed vessel on the Shabbat day. No man shall carry medicine on his person when going out or coming in on the Shabbat day.

15 In a dwelling place let no man lift up a stone or dust. The wet nurse shall not carry a suckling infant to go in and out on the Shabbat day.

16 No man shall provoke his manservant or his maidservant or his hired man on the Shabbat day. No man shall help an animal to give birth on the Shabbat day. And if it falls into a pit or ditch, let him not raise it on the Shabbat day.

17 No man shall rest in a place near the Gentiles on the Shabbat day. No man shall profane the Shabbat for any wealth or gain.

18 And if any man falls into a place of water or into a place of fire, let no man bring him up with a ladder or a rope or any instrument on the Shabbat day.

19 No man shall offer a whole burnt offering on the Shabbat except the Shabbat whole burnt offering, for so it is written: Besides your Shabbatot.

20 No man shall send a gift to the altar on the Shabbat except the Shabbat offering, for so Creator has commanded: These shall you offer to Creator on your appointed times. The rest is for the people.

21 And anyone who does not observe these things and these ordinances on the Shabbat day shall be cut off from the community. For the Shabbat day is the covenant sign between Creator and Yisra'EL forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran 4QD fragments confirm Column 10's Shabbat legislation. The Shabbat laws of the Damascus Document are among the most extensive and detailed in any Second Temple Jewish document. The covenant limit of one thousand cubits — verse 9 — for walking beyond the city on the Shabbat is the Damascus Document's specific covenant interpretation. The Community Rule and the Temple Scroll preserve related but sometimes distinct covenant Shabbat covenant regulations, demonstrating the covenant community's ongoing covenant engagement with the covenant precise covenant definition of covenant Shabbat covenant observance.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez Ethiopian covenant tradition preserves strong Shabbat covenant observance as a covenant central covenant mark of covenant covenant identity — the Ethiopian Beta Yisra'EL community's covenant Shabbat covenant practice is one of the most extensive in any covenant community outside the strict Qumran covenant tradition. The Damascus Document's covenant meticulous Shabbat covenant legislation reflects the same covenant conviction that the covenant Shabbat is the covenant foundational covenant sign of the covenant covenant relationship between Creator and Yisra'EL.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Shemot 20:8-11 and 31:12-17 provide the covenant foundational covenant Shabbat covenant legislation that Column 10 elaborates. The Damascus Document's covenant specific covenant distance rules, covenant carrying prohibitions, and covenant animal covenant regulations all develop the covenant general covenant Mosaic covenant Shabbat covenant framework into the covenant precise covenant communal covenant applications required by the covenant community's covenant daily covenant life.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 18 — if any man falls into a place of water or into a place of fire, let no man bring him up with a ladder or a rope or any instrument on the Shabbat day. This regulation — one of the most stringent in the Damascus Document's Shabbat laws — represents the covenant community's covenant conviction that the covenant Shabbat covenant prohibition admits no exceptions even in life-threatening situations. This position is more stringent than most covenant rabbinic covenant interpretation and reflects the covenant community's covenant absolute covenant commitment to the covenant literal covenant observance of the covenant Shabbat covenant prohibition.

DAMASCUS DOCUMENT — COLUMN 11 Further Shabbat Laws — Purity Laws for the Camp — No Man Shall Lie With a Woman in the City of the Sanctuary — The Laws Concerning the Priests — The Rule for the Assembly of the Cities — Concerning Testimony — No Man Shall Defile His Holy Spirit

1 And this is the rule for the assembly of the cities of Yisra'EL. According to all these ordinances, to distinguish between the clean and the unclean and to make known between the holy and the common.

2 No man shall lie with a woman in the city of the sanctuary to defile the city of the sanctuary with their uncleanness. And every man who approaches his wife for sex — as it is written: male and female he created them — he is unclean until evening.

3 He shall not touch any consecrated food. And if he has an emission of seed, he shall be unclean from the day of the emission until he has washed himself in water. And he shall not touch any consecrated food until the sun goes down.

4 No man shall bring any unclean animal or bird into the sanctuary. No man shall bring any leavened bread or old leaven into the sanctuary.

5 No man shall bring any unclean thing into the house of the sanctuary. Any man who has sexual intercourse with his wife and has not washed himself after having lain with her shall be unclean until he has washed himself.

6 Any man who eats flesh that has not been slaughtered in the community shall be unclean. Any man who touches the garments of a man with an emission shall be unclean.

7 These are the ordinances which every member of the covenant must observe according to the testimony of the Torah. And the Inspector shall explain these to all those who enter the congregation.

8 And this is the rule for the many: when they assemble together, everyone shall be seated in his place. The priests first, then the elders, then all the rest of the people. And in this order shall they be consulted on any matter.

9 And the priest who is appointed to oversee the many shall be examined concerning his understanding and he shall speak. But if the knowledge of anyone better is established before him, no man shall change his place.

10 And if a man has a matter to say before the assembly, no man shall speak before him except the priest or the Inspector. And the man who has the matter shall say: I have a matter. And the Inspector shall give him leave.

11 And no man shall eat and drink and engage in worldly conversation at the session of the many except according to this rule: no woman and no boy shall enter the assembly of the many. And no man of those who is smitten with any uncleanness that makes him impure shall enter the assembly.

12 And everyone who has not been inscribed in the register of the congregation by the Inspector — whether he is a wise man or a fool — shall not enter the assembly of the many.

13 And all who despise the ordinances of the covenant shall be judged and cut off from the congregation. Let no man eat with him. And let no man ask him a question about any matter.

14 Anyone who accuses his neighbor without just cause shall be excluded from the pure meal for a year. And all who are wrathful and speak arrogantly against the covenant shall be cut off from the midst of the congregation.

15 And anyone who speaks arrogantly against the priestly ordinances shall be excluded from the pure meal for a period of one year. And if anyone who is smitten with any uncleanness that makes him impure puts his hand to the pure food of the many, he shall be cut off from the congregation.

16 And anyone who keeps a grudge against his neighbor in violation of the commandment shall also be penalized in the same way. And no man shall bring an accusation before the assembly without two witnesses who are reliable and faithful.

17 And the witnesses must have seen it with their own eyes. And if a man says something that he has heard from a friend, he shall not be believed except by the mouth of two reliable witnesses.

18 And in capital cases, no accusation shall be brought except by three witnesses. And if these three witnesses are found to be faithful and reliable, the case shall be judged accordingly.

19 And no man shall touch the property of Creator and no man shall make himself ritually impure for the sake of his wealth. These are the exact interpretations of the Torah which every member of the congregation must observe.

20 And no man shall defile his holy spirit. For Creator separated them from all the people and commanded them to walk in his covenant and they were obligated to observe his Torah.

21 And Creator has not abandoned them and has not forgotten them but remembers his covenant with the forefathers. And they are the men who hold fast to his covenant in the time of his visitation.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran 4QD fragments confirm Column 11. The purity legislation governing sexual relations and their aftermath — verses 2-6 — reflects the Damascus Document's covenant application of the covenant Levitical covenant purity framework to the specific covenant community setting of the city-based camps rather than the Qumran desert covenant settlement. The covenant distinction between the city of the sanctuary and the broader land is the covenant community's covenant recognition of the covenant different covenant purity requirements for different covenant geographical covenant contexts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez covenant tradition preserves extensive covenant purity covenant legislation concerning sexual relations and post-coital covenant impurity that aligns with the Damascus Document's covenant framework. The covenant impurity until evening after sexual relations — verse 2-3 — is the covenant basic covenant Levitical covenant standard. The covenant additional covenant requirement of not touching consecrated food until bathing reflects the covenant community's covenant heightened covenant sensitivity to covenant ritual covenant purity boundaries.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Vayikra 15 provides the covenant foundational covenant purity legislation for Column 11's covenant sexual covenant purity rules. The Damascus Document's covenant specific covenant application — no man shall lie with a woman in the city of the sanctuary — extends the covenant Levitical covenant framework by creating a covenant geographical covenant zone of heightened covenant purity around the covenant sanctuary city that does not exist explicitly in the covenant Mosaic covenant text itself.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 20 — no man shall defile his holy spirit. For Creator separated them from all the people and commanded them to walk in his covenant. The covenant holy spirit — ruakh kodesh — within each covenant member of the congregation is the covenant internal covenant dimension of the covenant covenant identity that corresponds to the covenant external covenant ritual covenant purity requirements. The covenant defilement of the covenant holy spirit within is the covenant deeper covenant violation that the covenant external covenant purity covenant legislation is designed to protect against.

DAMASCUS DOCUMENT — COLUMN 12 No Man Shall Eat Anything That Has Not Been Slaughtered — The Laws of Commerce With Gentiles — No Man Shall Send the Son of a Stranger — Concerning the Sabbath Observance — Concerning the Unclean Bird — Concerning Fish — The Rule for the City of the Sanctuary

1 No man shall eat any living creature or any creeping thing that has not been slaughtered. For it is written: The life is in the blood. And whoever eats blood, whether from animals or from birds, defiles himself.

2 No man shall eat locusts of any kind except as they have been prepared alive, for this is the law for them. And no man shall defile his soul with any living creature or creeping creature, whether flies or wasps.

3 For they are among the creeping things that swarm. But fish shall not be eaten unless they have been split open alive and their blood poured out.

4 And all the locusts according to their kinds shall be put into fire or water while they are alive, for this is the law of their creation.

5 And all the wood and the stones and the dust which are defiled by the impurity of a man, by the impurity of oil upon them, shall be declared unclean according to their impurity. And the man who touches them shall be unclean accordingly.

6 And every vessel, nail, or peg in the wall shall be unclean with the same impurity as the vessel used for work.

7 The rule for the session of the cities of Yisra'EL: no man shall be alone with any woman. And if he is alone with her by chance in a field, in the open country or at night, it shall be reported to the Inspector.

8 And no man shall have intercourse with a woman except according to the rule of the covenant. And this applies also to all who enter his holy congregation. Any man who marries a woman who is unfit to enter the congregation shall be cut off from the midst of the congregation.

9 And no man shall bring into the congregation anyone who does not belong to the covenant or anyone who is smitten with an affliction that makes him unclean. And no man shall bring a stranger into the sanctuary.

10 And if a man sells a servant who is entered in the covenant and is faithful in observing its ordinances, his master shall not sell him to the Gentiles, for Creator alone is the master of all flesh.

11 And if anyone sets free from his master a servant who is entered in the covenant, the servant shall not be sold to the Gentiles without the agreement of the congregation.

12 And no man shall enter into an agreement or partnership with any Gentile in any matter of Torah or property. Nor shall he accept from them any food or drink, except for a price.

13 And no man shall eat or drink with them, or take from them anything that is forbidden, for whoever does these things makes himself unclean, and his defilement is upon him.

14 Anyone who commits adultery with his neighbor's wife, so as to make her unclean, shall be put to death. And any woman who brings any man to a foreign husband shall also be put to death.

15 The men of perfect holiness — concerning them it is written in the book of Yesha'yahu the son of Amotz the prophet: All who are enrolled for life in Yerushalayim, that is, those who are inscribed for eternal life.

16 And concerning those who treat the covenant with contempt: they are among those of whom Yekhezkel the prophet spoke in the words: The wicked man shall die because of his iniquity.

17 And whoever receives a man smitten with one of the afflictions described by Mosheh shall report it to the Inspector, and the Inspector shall investigate the case and examine the man who is smitten.

18 And if the Inspector finds that the man is smitten with the plague described in the words of Mosheh, he shall send him out of the congregation and he shall not return until he is healed.

19 And the Inspector shall not accept a man smitten with any affliction that defiles him into the congregation until the healing has occurred. And no man smitten with the plague of Mosheh shall enter the congregation.

20 These are the exact interpretations of the Torah for the assembly of the cities. And every man of the congregation who enters any of them shall be examined concerning their condition according to the interpretation of the Torah.

21 And the congregation shall walk in the exact interpretation of the Torah for all the time of their sojourning in the land of Damascus until the coming of the end of time for which the covenant was made.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran 4QD fragments confirm Column 12. The dietary legislation of verses 1-4 reflects the Damascus Document's covenant extension of the covenant Levitical covenant food laws to include specific covenant regulations about covenant fish — verse 3: fish must be split open alive and their blood poured out — and covenant locusts — verse 4: they must be put into fire or water while alive. These covenant specific covenant applications are not in the covenant Mosaic covenant Torah text but represent the covenant community's covenant careful covenant elaboration of the covenant underlying covenant principles.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez Ethiopian covenant dietary tradition includes strict covenant regulations on covenant blood — verse 1 — that align with the Damascus Document's covenant understanding. The covenant prohibition against eating blood as the covenant life-substance of the animal is a covenant foundational covenant covenant principle in both the Orit Ge'ez covenant tradition and the Damascus Document's covenant legal covenant framework. The covenant fish covenant regulation — covenant splitting open alive — is distinctive to the Damascus Document.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Vayikra 11 and Devarim 14 provide the covenant foundational covenant dietary covenant framework for Column 12. The Damascus Document's covenant specific covenant applications — particularly concerning fish preparation — extend beyond the explicit covenant Mosaic covenant legislation. The covenant citation of Yesha'yahu in verse 15 and Yekhezkel in verse 16 demonstrates the covenant community's covenant characteristic method of supporting covenant legal covenant positions with covenant prophetic covenant citation.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 21 — the congregation shall walk in the exact interpretation of the Torah for all the time of their sojourning in the land of Damascus until the coming of the end of time for which the covenant was made. The covenant temporal covenant horizon of the entire Laws section: the covenant specific covenant regulations are not permanent covenant ideal covenant legislation but covenant emergency covenant measures for the covenant age of wickedness — the covenant interim period between the covenant community's covenant founding and the covenant eschatological covenant end of time for which the covenant covenant was made.

DAMASCUS DOCUMENT — COLUMN 13 The Rule of the Inspector of the Camp — He Shall Instruct the Many — He Shall Examine All Who Enter His Congregation — He Shall Carry Them Like a Shepherd — No Member Shall Bring Into the Congregation Those Outside the Covenant — The Inspector Shall Perform the Examination

1 And this is the rule for the Inspector of the camp. He shall instruct the many in the works of Creator and make them understand his mighty deeds and recount to them what happened from everlasting.

2 And he shall love them as a father loves his children and shall carry all their distress as a shepherd carries his flock. And he shall loosen all the fetters that bind them so that there shall be neither oppressed nor afflicted in his congregation.

3 And everyone who joins his congregation, he shall examine him concerning his deeds, his understanding, his strength, his power, and his wealth. And he shall inscribe him in his place according to his share in the lot of the truth.

4 And no member of the camp shall have authority to admit anyone to the congregation against the judgment of the Inspector of the camp. And no one of those who have entered the covenant of Creator shall deal with them.

5 And anyone who enters the congregation to walk according to the exact interpretation of the Torah, if anyone does any of the works of God apart from the exact interpretation of the Torah, the Inspector shall act accordingly.

6 And the Inspector shall examine the matters and shall inform the offender of his transgression. And the Inspector shall rebuke him in love and shall not bear hatred against him.

7 And the Inspector shall not set his heart against anyone or be wrathful toward anyone except against those who transgress the covenant willfully. And no man shall be put to shame before his neighbor in public.

8 And the Inspector shall rebuke him in humility of spirit and with love. And if a man sins before him in a matter of the Torah, he shall correct him immediately.

9 No man of all those who enter the covenant of the community shall conduct business with his neighbor. And no man shall bring into the pure food of the congregation anyone except those who are pure.

10 And the Inspector shall examine all who enter the congregation year by year to see whether their walk is according to the Torah. And anyone who is found to have broken the exact interpretation of the Torah shall be cut off from the pure food of the congregation.

11 No man shall speak a word that is contrary to the Torah or raise his voice against the members of the congregation or shout or speak harshly to the Inspector. And anyone who does any of these things shall be penalized for a period of one year.

12 No man shall spread evil reports against his neighbor. No man shall tell anything to one who does not know the Torah in order to injure his neighbor. No man shall engage in any unclean business.

13 And no man shall speak with his mouth anything that is vain or foolish or empty. No man shall laugh foolishly or raise his voice in laughter on the Shabbat or on any appointed feast.

14 Anyone who does any of these things shall be penalized for thirty days. And if anyone takes anything from the common property without the authority of the Inspector, he shall be penalized for sixty days.

15 Anyone who strikes his neighbor in anger without proper cause shall be penalized for six months. And anyone who takes revenge into his own hands against the members of the congregation for any matter shall be penalized for one year.

16 And no man shall bring the name of the Creator who is over all — who is honored and feared and terrible — on his lips or on his tongue. And anyone who swears or curses while reading the book of the Torah or at prayer shall be excluded from the congregation.

17 And all those who enter after him into the congregation shall be instructed in the exact interpretation of the Torah according to the rule that the Inspector has established for the many.

18 And the Inspector shall teach them the exact interpretation of the Torah and the precepts and the judgments and the regulations and all the ordinances of the community, to walk in them in the time of wickedness.

19 And this is the rule concerning the money of the congregation: everyone who is enrolled in the congregation shall bring at least two days' wages each month into the hand of the Inspector and the judges.

20 And from it they shall give to the orphans and shall support the poor and the needy and the old man who is dying and to the man who is taken captive by a foreign people and to the girl who has no near kinsman and to the girl whom no man desires to marry.

21 And all the works of the congregation shall be done in truth and righteousness and humility before Creator. And in all their councils they shall act according to these rules throughout all the time of their sojourning.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran 4QD fragments confirm Column 13. The covenant role of the Inspector — mevakker — is described here in the greatest covenant detail in the Damascus Document: he instructs, he examines covenant members annually, he performs pastoral covenant care, he maintains covenant community finances, and he administers covenant discipline. The covenant Inspector is simultaneously covenant teacher, covenant administrator, covenant judge, and covenant shepherd — the covenant most comprehensive covenant leadership role in the covenant community's covenant institutional structure.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez covenant tradition does not preserve the Damascus Document. However, the covenant community welfare system described in verses 19-20 — supporting orphans, the poor, the needy, the elderly, the captive, and the unmarried girl — reflects the same covenant covenant social covenant ethic that the Orit Ge'ez preserves in its covenant treatment of the covenant Mosaic covenant social covenant legislation. The covenant two-days-wages monthly contribution is the covenant community's covenant practical covenant implementation of the covenant covenant obligation to care for the covenant vulnerable.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic covenant social covenant legislation — Shemot 22:22, Devarim 14:29, 24:17-22 — provides the covenant foundational covenant obligations that Column 13 institutionalizes. The Damascus Document's covenant specific covenant monthly contribution system — verse 19 — is the covenant community's covenant answer to the covenant question of how the covenant Mosaic covenant covenant social covenant obligations can be fulfilled in a covenant community organized around covenant camp life rather than covenant agricultural covenant life.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — he shall love them as a father loves his children and shall carry all their distress as a shepherd carries his flock. The covenant double metaphor for the Inspector's covenant pastoral covenant role — father and shepherd — is the covenant most theologically rich covenant description of covenant leadership in the Damascus Document. The covenant father's covenant love and the covenant shepherd's covenant care are presented as the covenant two covenant dimensions of the covenant authentic covenant leadership: covenant affective covenant commitment and covenant active covenant service.

DAMASCUS DOCUMENT — COLUMN 14 The Rule for the Assembly — Each Man Shall Be Seated in His Place — The Priests First — No Boy Shall Enter the Assembly — The Rule for the Judges of the Congregation — Ten Men Selected From the Congregation — Four From the Tribe of Levi and Aharon

1 This is the rule for the session of the many. All those who are enrolled shall be present. The priests first, then the elders, then all the people, each in his turn.

2 And thus shall they be consulted in every matter. And the priest who is at the head of the many shall be examined as to his understanding, and he shall speak to the many. And then the elders shall speak.

3 And no man shall interrupt the speech of his neighbor before his brother finishes speaking. And no man shall speak before his turn, according to his rank.

4 And no boy shall enter the session of the many. And no man who is foolish and simple-minded or is smitten with any impurity shall enter the session of the many.

5 And this is the rule for the judges of the congregation. Their number shall be ten men selected from the congregation, four from the tribe of Levi and Aharon, and six from Yisra'EL.

6 They shall be learned in the book of HAGY and in the principles of the covenant. They shall be between twenty-five and sixty years of age. And no one over sixty years of age shall hold the office of judge of the congregation.

7 For because of man's sin, his days have been shortened. And in the heat of the anger of Creator against the inhabitants of the earth he commanded to remove from the service of the congregation those whose spirit has departed from Creator before they complete their days.

8 And this is the rule concerning losses. If a man loses cattle or any other kind of property at the hand of a neighbor, the neighbor shall restore it in full.

9 If he cannot restore it in full, the judges shall have him brought and they shall examine his property. And they shall order him to restore it to the owner at whatever it is worth.

10 And if he does not have the means, his property shall be seized as surety until such time as he can repay the debt. And in the case of those who are incapable, the matter shall be brought before the congregation.

11 And the Inspector shall give from the funds of the congregation to anyone smitten with affliction who is without means. And he shall report the matter to the congregation when they meet together.

12 And this is the rule for the covenant: whenever there is a grievance of a man against his neighbor, it shall be brought before the Inspector, and the Inspector shall decide between them in righteousness and truth.

13 And if a man has a grievance against his neighbor, it shall be settled before the court. And the judges shall not favor either party, and they shall not take gifts or bribes in any matter.

14 And whoever despises or dishonors the judges shall be excluded from the pure food of the congregation and penalized for a period of one year. And if the offender is a priest, he shall not minister at the altar.

15 And the Inspector who is over all the camps shall be between forty and fifty years of age. He shall be master of all the secret of men and of every language. And according to his word shall the members of the congregation come in and go out, each according to his rank.

16 And no man shall do business with his neighbor except through the Inspector. And no man shall form a partnership with anyone outside the covenant except by hand to hand.

17 And all those who walk in these things with a perfect heart shall be established in the covenant forever and shall atone for the land and shall render judgment upon the wicked and there shall be no injustice with them.

18 And when the end of time comes, the members of the covenant shall be gathered together and shall bring him an offering of incense and a burnt offering and all the sacrifices which Creator ordained for the appointed time.

19 And the congregation shall not depart from these rules in any matter until the day when there arises the Teacher of Righteousness at the end of days. For these are the rules by which they shall walk in the time of wickedness.

20 And whoever enters the covenant with his whole heart and all his soul shall keep all the commandments of Creator and shall not rebel against them on any pretext or for any reason whatsoever.

21 And the covenant of the fathers shall be renewed for them. And they shall walk in all his ways and shall not depart from the covenant of love and truth which Creator made with the forefathers.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran 4QD fragments confirm Column 14. The covenant judicial structure of verses 5-6 — ten judges, four from the tribe of Levi and Aharon, six from Yisra'EL, between twenty-five and sixty years of age — is the Damascus Document's concrete covenant institutional answer to the covenant question of how covenant justice is administered in the covenant camps. The covenant age limits — verses 6-7 — reflect the covenant community's covenant interpretation of covenant human covenant lifespan in the covenant age of wickedness.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez covenant tradition preserves a strong covenant emphasis on covenant judicial covenant integrity — no favoritism, no bribes — verse 13 — that aligns with the Damascus Document's covenant judicial covenant regulations. The covenant Orit Ge'ez Devarim covenant tradition's covenant passionate concern for covenant judicial covenant impartiality is here institutionalized in the covenant specific covenant structural covenant requirements of the Damascus Document's covenant court system.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 16:18-20 and 17:8-13 provide the covenant foundational covenant judicial covenant framework for Column 14. The covenant Damascus Document's covenant court of ten judges with the specified covenant tribal covenant distribution — four from Levi and Aharon, six from Yisra'EL — is the covenant community's covenant translation of the covenant Mosaic covenant judicial covenant structure into the specific covenant institutional covenant requirements of the covenant camp context.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 19 — the congregation shall not depart from these rules in any matter until the day when there arises the Teacher of Righteousness at the end of days. The covenant eschatological covenant qualification of the entire Laws section: the covenant specific covenant regulations of the Damascus Document are not the covenant permanent covenant ideal but the covenant emergency covenant legislation for the covenant age of wickedness. The covenant Teacher of Righteousness who arises at the end of days will bring the covenant definitive covenant Torah interpretation that supersedes the covenant interim covenant measures.

DAMASCUS DOCUMENT — COLUMN 15 The Rule for the Oath of Entry Into the Covenant — No Man Shall Admit Anyone Who Is Below Twenty Years of Age — The Oath of the Covenant Is Binding — Anyone Who Violates the Oath Shall Be Cut Off — The One Who Enters Shall Take the Oath Before the Inspector — He Shall Be Accepted According to His Understanding

1 And this is the rule for the oath of entry into the covenant of the community. He shall swear an oath by the covenant to return with his whole heart and with all his soul to the Torah of Mosheh according to all that was commanded and shall not depart from it for any reason.

2 For anyone who departs from the covenant of Mosheh either overtly or covertly shall not be accepted into the holy congregation even if he returns. And he shall not be inscribed in the register of the congregation.

3 No man shall enter into a covenant or conduct business with any man outside the covenant in the matter of covenant property or in the matter of covenant law.

4 But he shall keep away from all the property of uncleanness and shall not touch it or take anything from it. And he shall abstain from the wealth of wickedness which defiles.

5 Whoever is with his master, whether in the household or in the field, whether one of the sons of the covenant or of those who walk in the way of holiness — shall not bring him any food or any legal matter.

6 And when the Inspector receives a man in the covenant of the many, he shall instruct him in the ordinances of the covenant and establish him in the understanding of all its rules. And he shall inscribe him in his place according to his lot.

7 And he shall examine him according to his understanding and his works. And according to his lot in the congregation, so shall he be ranked in the order of the community.

8 And no man shall enter the covenant on the day of his being inscribed, if he is smitten with any affliction or is unclean according to the ordinances of the Torah, and whoever does not know these ordinances shall not enter until they are taught to him.

9 And no man below twenty years of age shall be inscribed in the covenant to know good and evil. And no man who is listed in the ordinances of the covenant shall receive any judgment from them except he knows it well.

10 And no man below twenty years of age shall be reckoned in the holy congregation, for the children of Yisra'EL did not know good and evil until the age of twenty.

11 And whoever has entered the covenant according to these rules shall submit to the Inspector's examination each year. And according to his understanding and his works in the Torah, so shall his place be raised or lowered.

12 And each man shall rebuke his neighbor in truth and humility and brotherly love. And no man shall speak to his neighbor in anger or in a complaining voice. And no man shall hate his neighbor.

13 For the one who hates his neighbor without rebuking him has allowed wickedness to be upon him. And the covenant requires that before bearing a testimony against anyone, he shall rebuke him privately first.

14 And the one who is rebuked shall not be angry with the one who rebukes him. And if he is angry with him and speaks against him, he has transgressed the covenant, and they shall penalize him according to the ordinances.

15 And no man shall raise his hand against the life of his neighbor except for legitimate judgment according to the laws of the covenant.

16 And anyone who brings a false accusation against his neighbor shall be penalized for a full year. And he shall be separated from the pure food of the many for six months. And the matter shall be recorded in the register before the Inspector.

17 And no man shall take revenge by his own hand or hold a grudge against the members of the covenant but shall reckon from day to day according to the ordinances.

18 No man shall do anything among the members of the congregation that is not commanded. No man shall do any abomination or any work of creation on the Shabbat or on the Feast day.

19 These are all the ordinances by which the men of perfect holiness shall walk, one with another, and they shall be separated from every man who does not walk in these ways.

20 And the congregation of the holy ones shall walk in them when the Messiah of Aharon and Yisra'EL comes, and he shall atone for their transgression and their sin. And these are the rules for the congregation of the perfect holiness.

21 And all those who enter the covenant of the community in the land of Damascus shall be established in the covenant forever according to all these rules, and they shall walk in the ways of the Creator of heaven and earth in truth.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran 4QD fragments confirm Column 15's covenant entry regulations. The covenant oath of entry — verses 1-2 — is the covenant formal covenant act by which an individual becomes a covenant member of the Damascus Document covenant community. The covenant irrevocability of the covenant oath — whoever departs shall not be accepted back even if he returns, verse 2 — is one of the most stringent covenant membership covenant conditions in the Qumran covenant literature. The covenant twenty-year minimum age for covenant entry — verses 9-10 — is the covenant community's covenant standard for covenant moral covenant maturity.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez covenant tradition does not directly address covenant community covenant entry regulations. However, the covenant emphasis on covenant complete covenant heart-submission to the covenant Torah of Mosheh — verse 1: with his whole heart and with all his soul — reflects the covenant Devarim 6:5 covenant Shema framework that both the Orit Ge'ez and the Damascus Document treat as the covenant foundational covenant orientation of covenant covenant life.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 6:5 and 10:16 provide the covenant foundational covenant texts for the covenant whole-heart-and-soul covenant commitment of verse 1. The covenant Damascus Document's covenant entry oath establishes covenant formal covenant institutional covenant structures for what the covenant Mosaic covenant Torah presents as the covenant basic covenant covenant orientation of every covenant Israelite: returning with the whole heart and all the soul to the Torah of Mosheh.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 20 — when the Messiah of Aharon and Yisra'EL comes, and he shall atone for their transgression and their sin. The covenant reappearance of the covenant Messiah of Aharon and Yisra'EL — the covenant dual-Messiah figure — at the covenant climax of the Laws section confirms that the entire covenant legislative structure of the Damascus Document is understood as covenant interim covenant legislation pending the covenant messianic covenant arrival. The covenant Messiah's covenant atoning function at his covenant coming is the covenant eschatological covenant resolution of all the covenant community's covenant accumulated covenant transgressions during the covenant age of wickedness.

DAMASCUS DOCUMENT — COLUMN 16 The Book of the Divisions of the Times — The Book of HAGY — The Exact Interpretation of the Covenant — Vows and Oaths — No Man Shall Vow to the Altar Anything Acquired by Violence — Anyone Who Vows By the Torah of Mosheh Shall Not Revoke It — MS B Closes

1 And this is the exact interpretation of the Torah which they shall observe in the land of Damascus according to the book of the divisions of the times, in their Jubilees and in their weeks.

2 And on the day that a man swears to return to the Torah of Mosheh, the angel Mastema shall turn aside from him if he keeps his word. This is why Avraham was circumcised on the day that he had knowledge of it.

3 And concerning the word which it says: That which passes from your lips you shall observe and perform: every binding oath which a man swears to do something according to the Torah, let him not revoke it even at the cost of death.

4 Whatever a man swears to depart from the Torah, let him not make it binding upon him even if it costs him a fine. He shall confess to the priest and atone.

5 And no man shall make a vow to the altar from anything acquired by violence. And the priests shall not receive from anyone anything acquired by violence.

6 And no man shall sanctify the food of his table to Creator, for this is what it says: They trap each other in a net.

7 And no man shall consecrate anything that pertains to the altar without the knowledge of the priest, for whoever consecrates without the knowledge of the priest shall be liable to a penalty.

8 And if anyone consecrates his field or his vineyard or any of his possessions, the priest shall examine it and establish the value, and the owner shall take it back at the price that the priest has established.

9 And let no man consecrate his house to Creator without the knowledge of the priest and without the assembly. And whoever consecrates without their knowledge has consecrated nothing.

10 And whoever vows anything by the oath of the covenant shall not revoke it. And if he vows anything in error and says: I did not know what I was swearing — then he shall confess before the community and atone.

11 And the priest shall examine the vow, and if he finds that he has vowed in truth he shall establish the vow and it shall stand. And if it is in error, he shall release him from it.

12 And all these things shall apply to all who have entered the covenant in the land of Damascus. And they shall walk in them according to the exact interpretation of the Torah for all the time of their sojourning in the land of wickedness.

13 And as for their wealth, any man who gives of his wealth to the holy council shall not transfer it to another. And no man shall give of the holy property of the sanctuary to any man or any creature except the priests and the Levites.

14 And no one shall bring any impure thing into the assembly of the holy ones. And anyone who does so shall be cut off from the congregation and from the lot of the holy ones.

15 And these are the rules for the men of perfect holiness who walk in these things. And anyone who departs from them in any matter shall be judged. This is the rule for the whole congregation of Yisra'EL in the end of days.

16 And anyone who enters the covenant of the many — whoever it may be — must do so by oath. And the oath must be made before the whole assembly of the community and before the Inspector.

17 And the Inspector shall accept him and inscribe him in the register of the congregation. And he shall be inscribed according to his understanding and his works. And according to his lot in the congregation, so shall he be ranked in the order of the community.

18 And he shall swear with the oath of the covenant to return to the Torah of Mosheh with his whole heart and with all his soul, to do what he is commanded to do according to all that is revealed of it to the sons of Tzadok, the priests who keep the covenant.

19 And these are the rules which the wise men of the community shall follow in order to walk in the paths of truth and righteousness. They shall not lean on the works of their own hands nor on the stubbornness of their heart.

20 For these are the rules for the men of perfect holiness whose covenant Creator established with Yisra'EL forever, that they might inherit the earth and walk in the perfect way in all the things of the earth according to the will of Creator.

21 And the covenant of Creator stands fast forever. And these are the men of the name who are called by name and who shall arise at the end of days. Blessed is the one who does them and who does not defile himself. For him shall there be life forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran 4QD fragments confirm Column 16, which concludes the Laws section of the Damascus Document. The covenant reference to the book of the divisions of the times in their Jubilees and in their weeks — verse 1 — directly connects the Damascus Document to the Yovelim covenant text: the covenant community's covenant calendar is grounded in the covenant Jubilee covenant framework that Yovelim establishes. The covenant citation of this covenant calendar-book as a covenant legal covenant authority demonstrates the covenant inseparability of the Damascus Document's covenant legal covenant program from the covenant calendar covenant theology of the covenant community.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves the book of Yovelim in full — referenced in verse 1 as the book of the divisions of the times in their Jubilees — as part of the Ethiopian canonical collection. This covenant canonical covenant connection between the Damascus Document and Yovelim confirms the covenant significance of Yovelim as the covenant foundational covenant calendar covenant text for the broader Qumran-Ethiopian covenant covenant tradition. The covenant calendar framework of Yovelim underpins the Damascus Document's covenant appointed times covenant legislation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic covenant tradition does not include Yovelim in its canonical collection. The Damascus Document's citation of Yovelim as a covenant legal authority — placing it alongside the covenant Torah of Mosheh — is the covenant Qumran community's covenant canonical covenant affirmation that Yovelim carries covenant legislative covenant weight equivalent to the covenant Mosaic covenant Torah for the covenant community's covenant life.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 21 — the covenant of Creator stands fast forever. And these are the men of the name who are called by name and who shall arise at the end of days. Blessed is the one who does them and who does not defile himself. For him shall there be life forever. The covenant closing covenant benediction of the Damascus Document is simultaneously the covenant conclusion of the Laws and the covenant echo of the Admonition's covenant opening promise: the covenant men called by name — verse 1:17 — who shall arise at the end of days inherit the covenant final covenant promise of the entire document: life forever — khayyei netzakh.

THE DAMASCUS DOCUMENT — COMPLETE

Volume Thirty — The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

BLESSED IS THE ONE WHO DOES THEM AND WHO DOES NOT DEFILE HIMSELF

FOR HIM SHALL THERE BE LIFE FOREVER

Damascus Document Column 16
